segments.text: Hi everyone, I'm Fabio from Free Wings and today we're going to look at meteorology together.
This is mainly about exam preparation, or about meteo basics in general.
For me, there are two ways to approach learning meteorology.
One is the minimum-effort approach — just getting through the theory exam somehow.
And then of course there's a lot more weather knowledge you can teach and learn.
There are basically no limits there — you could spend five days just talking about meteorology.
But this course really focuses on the basics.
It's also suitable for experienced pilots,
to refresh their weather knowledge a bit or learn some new things.
There are definitely some interesting topics in here for experienced pilots too.
But this course is of course mainly designed for flight schools,
so we can hopefully give you a good grasp of weather knowledge —
stuff that might be rather dry in the theory book and a bit hard to understand.
And so let's dive into the fascinating field of weather knowledge.
One thing that's always very close to my heart, especially in meteorology,
is that there are basically two sides to it.
On one hand you have theoretical weather knowledge, where you can learn a lot and understand a lot of theory.
But in meteorology, the weather happens out there in the open.
It has a lot to do with experience — flight practice or hands-on meteo practice.
A lot of it is about observing the weather over and over, analyzing it,
and constantly matching theory with practice.
But in this section, it's mainly about theory and building a basic understanding of the weather.
Let's start with the biggest picture. Where does the weather actually happen?
The atmosphere around the world is divided into different layers.
But the most relevant one for us is the troposphere,
which is bounded at the top by what's called the tropopause.
And this lower layer is where most of the humidity is found,
most of the water vapour — and so it's what determines our weather.
When you look from space, from a space station,
it's just a very thin layer that you can see here.
In scale, it's about as thin as an onion skin.
It's really a very, very thin band where weather phenomena take place,
and of course where we move around too.
And we, as paraglider pilots, actually only move in the lowest layer of this troposphere.
Something important from the theory — you also need to know this for the theory exam —
the composition of the gases in this troposphere.
There's the 21% oxygen, which is of course what we need to breathe,
78% nitrogen, and then there's 1% noble gas,
which is also part of the mix.
But for us, for meteorology, that's not of much relevance —
the remaining noble gases in there.
The troposphere isn't equally thick all the way around the globe.
It's a bit thinner at the poles than at the equator.
That has to do with the Earth's rotation.
At the equator it's about 18 km, and at the poles around 8 km.
Here in Europe, at our latitude where we do most of our flying,
the troposphere is about 11 to 13 km thick.
You have to picture it — 13,000 metres.
Then we talk about the standard atmosphere.
This keeps coming up in flight weather, and especially in the theory exam.
They'll keep asking you about the standard atmosphere.
It's that atmosphere where the weather would be completely balanced out —
no high-pressure areas, no low-pressure areas,
and none of the various factors that influence our weather,
which we'll look at in a moment.
If everything balanced out, you'd get what's called the standard atmosphere.
It's like the zero point, where everything has evened out.
There are a few numbers and facts you need to know for the theory exam.
The important one is the temperature gradient with altitude —
We're talking about 6.5 degrees per 1000 metres,
or per 100 metres, that's 0.65 degrees per 100 metres.
At the Earth's surface, the standard atmosphere is 15 degrees,
and at the top of the troposphere it's minus 56 degrees.
The lower two numbers are especially important —
make sure you learn those for the theory exam.
The temperature gradient of course varies depending on the weather situation,
But in the standard atmosphere, as I said, it's 6.5 degrees per 100 metres.
In the standard atmosphere we also talk about a standard air pressure.
We can't really get into air pressure yet,
but the number we'll hear again and again
when the entire troposphere balances out, as I mentioned.

is at 11 kilometres in the standard atmosphere.

is how it drops with altitude.
